header.headline: Grouped Query Attention
header.subtitle: Share K/V heads across query groups to cut cache cost without collapsing the whole model into one KV pair.

left.title: MHA baseline
left.body: Every attention head gets its own K/V heads, which maximizes flexibility but grows cache and bandwidth.

right.title: GQA compromise
right.body: Queries stay numerous, but K/V heads are shared in groups for a better quality-cost tradeoff.

panels.title: Why not MQA?
panels.summary: Single-KV attention is cheaper still, but can erase too much representational flexibility.
panels.note: The sweet spot is not always the cheapest point.

panels.title: Shared KV
panels.summary: Multiple query heads point at the same K/V pair, so the cache scales with groups instead of heads.
panels.note: A structural change becomes a memory win.

panels.title: KV cache drop
panels.summary: Lower KV cardinality means less memory traffic and smaller serving footprint.
panels.note: Inference cost often follows cache shape.

callout.title: Key takeaway
points: GQA is a systems compromise, not just an architecture fad.
It preserves multiple queries while trimming cache cost.
Shared structure is exactly what the DSL should expose.
